summary:
Imhotep, who served as chief minister and architect to Pharaoh Djoser of the [[event:1712|Third Dynasty]] around 2670–2650 BC, is the earliest named architect and engineer in recorded history. He is credited with designing the revolutionary [[event:1709|Step Pyramid at Saqqara]], the world's first large-scale stone building, as well as the elaborate mortuary complex surrounding it. His achievement in conceiving and executing a monument of this scale from stone — rather than the mudbrick used in all previous major constructions — places him among the most consequential individuals in the history of architecture.

Beyond his architectural work, Imhotep held an extraordinary range of titles under Djoser, including High Priest of Heliopolis, Chancellor of the King of Lower Egypt, and Administrator of the Great Palace. He was also reputedly a physician, astronomer, and scribe, making him one of the earliest known polymath figures in history. Ancient Egyptian sources praised his wisdom and learning, and his reputation grew long after his death.

The posthumous veneration of Imhotep is a remarkable phenomenon in Egyptian religious history. Within a few centuries of his death he was being worshipped as a wise man and healer; by the Late Period (roughly 664–332 BC), he had been fully deified and was worshipped as a son of the god Ptah and as a divine patron of medicine and scribes. His cult centres at Memphis and Philae attracted pilgrims seeking cures for illness. Greek visitors identified him with their own god of medicine, Asclepius, creating one of the clearest examples of religious syncretism between Egyptian and Hellenic traditions.

Imhotep represents a uniquely important type of historical figure — a commoner (or at least non-royal) whose personal achievements were so exceptional that he transcended the boundaries of human status in the collective memory of his civilisation. His deification stands in contrast to the dominant Egyptian tradition of attributing great works entirely to the pharaoh, and it testifies to the extraordinary impression his intellect and talent made on those who knew him and on posterity.
